A 1010 foil usually gives you a really nice balance of:

• Easy pumping so you can connect waves
• Early lift which helps in knee to hip high surf
• Enough glide to keep speed through soft sections
• Still manageable at shoulder high without feeling too slow

Dropping to the 950 when the waves get a bit punchier makes sense. You normally get:

• More speed and tighter turning
• Less front wing drag
• Better control when the wave has more push

890 as the main wing for shoulder to head high waves makes a lot of sense. At that size you usually get:

• Much tighter turning radius
• More speed down the line
• Better control on steeper faces
• Less “over-foiling” feeling when the wave has power

And because you’re already an experienced foiler who likes linking waves and riding swell, the 890 is right in that sweet spot where it still pumps but feels really surfy.



1010
• Knee to waist high
• Weak / slow waves
• Maximum glide and pump for connecting waves

950
• Small but clean surf
• When you still want early lift but a bit more speed

890
• Shoulder to head high
• Best carving and most surf-like feeling

I mostly use the 780 for tow foiling at the moment and I really love it. It still holds a pretty good size of swell, feels super turny, and the glide is amazing as long as you keep the speed up. Especially on non breaking waves or rolling swell it’s insane.

I haven’t tried the 740 yet, but I did try the 830. For me the 830 just feels way too lifty. I would only use it in really small waves, like hip to maybe shoulder high fast waves. Otherwise it just feels like too much lift for what I like.

A few tips if you’re choosing a tow foil:

• Don’t go too big on the front wing. Bigger wings feel easier at the start but once the swell has energy they can feel really lifty and harder to control.
• Try to match the foil size to the speed of the swell, not just the wave height. Fast rolling swell usually feels better on a slightly smaller wing.
• Keep your speed up after you drop the rope. Smaller wings like the 780 really come alive when you carry speed and use the glide.
• If you want tighter turns and a more surfy feeling, going a little smaller on the front wing often makes a huge difference.

For me the 780 feels like a really nice balance between glide, speed, and turning.

For parawinging I use most of the time the 1010. It just gives you heaps of glide and makes the whole ride really easy, especially on longer runs where you want a bit of a break in between. You can just let it run and it keeps holding the swell without having to pump much.

If I’m doing shorter runs or want it to feel a bit more surfy and the swell is really cranking, then I go down to a 950 or even an 890. But most of the time I really enjoy the 1010 because it holds so much swell for downwinding on the Parawing. It gives you those little moments to relax between turns and just glide, which is super fun. I’m really loving that setup.

A few tips for others getting into Parawing downwinding:

• Don’t go too small on the foil at the start. A bit more surface like a 1300ish wing helps a lot with glide and connecting bumps.
• Focus on linking swell instead of pumping. Bigger wings make it easier to just read the ocean and flow from bump to bump.
• Choose the foil based on the energy of the swell. On strong windy days you can go smaller, but on weaker days the extra glide is gold.
• Let the foil work. Sometimes the best thing is to relax, keep your speed, and let the wing glide instead of forcing turns or pumps.

Once you get the feeling of just flowing with the swell, that’s when Parawinging becomes really addictive.

rear_wing: Skinny 35 but changing to skinny surf 43 (which work SO good with fireball 1250!!)
why:
For our conditions in Switzerland it’s the must have. You can turn and have a lot of glide in the wave. I use the same set up (1250) for pumping in the flat. 
For wakethiefing, I use the 1401 to assure my starts and catch the boat waves. With the weight of the board (SUP) it works good. But I plan to use also the 1250 on the flat days. I already use it on the small small days (quite small in Switzerland 😅) and it works already good.

It’s the setup for all conditions in pumping. You can start easily everywhere, pump mid distance, turn in the small waves, do a little bit of freestyle. I used to ride the skinny 35 with it but I tried the surf skinny 43, which totally unlocked my set up in powerful curves . I have much more back foot and can be more violent/ sharp when I turn. With the same turn, I would overfoil/fall with the skinny 35. What’s also interesting is that I also find the glide and the front projection, like the skinny 35. So it’s very good cause I found all the points I was searching for with a set up

It’s the setup for all conditions in pumping. You can start easily everywhere, pump mid distance, turn in the small waves, do a little bit of freestyle. I used to ride the skinny 35 with it but I tried the surf skinny 43, which totally unlocked my set up in powerful curves . I have much more back foot and can be more violent/ sharp when I turn. With the same turn, I would overfoil/fall with the skinny 35. What’s also interesting is that I also find the glide and the front projection, like the skinny 35. So it’s very good cause I found all the points I was searching for with a set up

When there’s some waves by us (strong wind), I use the artpro999 which allows me more fault with speed. I put the skinny 40 to increase  bit the drag and have more control. But it’s not the set up I use the most. Sometimes I use also the fireball 1250, which is really my allround wing
In small conditions, fireball goes well. The limit is 18-20knot in a 4m parading (for me). I also use the art 999 but I don’t do so much parawing. Much more SUP DW / SUP foiling
The glide is insane !

Efficiency and control
Can use it in most conditions
It’s incredibly fast, stiff and can keep up with open ocean swells and has plenty of low end for less than ideal days
I can take it down winding, it’s also my favorite in the surf. The wing turns incredibly well and has plenty of low end for me to link multiple waves together.

The 780 Surge is my go to for wing foiling. It has a phenomenally sharp turning. Pair this with the Ultra short fuse and a positive 1/2 degree shim plate with the progressive 280 tail and you still have great pitch stability for a smaller wing. Having the different options of fuse length, tail size, and shims can make you tailor your Axis setup for each riders personal preference and riding style. Definitely the coolest part of Axis is how many different combinations you can make with each front wing

I love how solid the 82cm HM Carbon mast is for tow foiling. I can exceed speeds of over 25 mph and never feel a wiggle in the mast. The fuselage connection is just as solid. The Surge 780 turns extremely well paired with the Crazyshort fuse, I add in a positive full degree shim plate to ensure pitch stability. I have been absolutely loving this setup while tow foiling!

What I love most about the Surge 890 while riding my SUP in the surf is the combination of glide and surfy feeling. I am able to rip turns and link waves for 10 plus minutes. Having the option for different fuse lengths and tail styles and sizes allow each rider to tailor a setup exactly for their own riding style. Personally using a Ultrashort fuse paired with a 300 progressive tail and a positive 1/2 degree shim with the 890 Surge has allowed for me to do exactly what I desire each session.

What I love most with this setup parawing is its ability to fly at high speeds and turning on a dime. Once I’m up on foil I’m able to stash the parawing and have full confidence in the low end of the front wing anywhere from 15-35 mph wind. I have used this setup in some rather large surf while on the parawing and it never ceases to amaze me.
The ability to ride efficiently and keep up faster moving bumps but has a pretty low stall speed. Basically has a huge range.
Pump and turning is great
Speed range is amazing and can go really fast

Range is great and when I pack away it pumps and ride faster moving bumps

Carving on wakesurf waves with this setup is incredibly fun. The shorter fuselage makes the foil more pitch-sensitive, but it also gives it a very lively and responsive feel. As a result, the foil turns extremely quickly and allows for tight, dynamic carves on the wave.

I’m really looking forward to testing the new Surge wings behind the wakeboat as well. Unfortunately, I haven’t had the opportunity yet, but I’m excited to see how they perform in that kind of wave.

With the 1500, I can easily do runs of over 10 minutes. It has incredible glide, and for a wing with a 1500 span it still turns surprisingly well. It even works in small waves.

With the bigger 1750, I managed to complete my one-hour challenge. It’s an amazing glider, but these wings are quite pitch-sensitive and not the easiest to start. However, once you manage the start, it feels like you can pump forever.

The PNG 1300 is one of the most versatile pump foil wings. It offers excellent glide, smooth and predictable turns, and performs surprisingly well even in small waves.

The Surge 1010 is better suited for shorter runs and riders who want to practice tight, aggressive turns. It’s incredibly fun and fast, but also more challenging for dock starts.

I don't have a lot of experience wingfoiling. And the biggest issue I have, right now, is that my beginner board has US rails that are too short. Consequently, with the advance+ fuselage configuration, I cannot put the mast in the front enough. Mast is always too much in the back (even when being set as much as possible to the front), and wingfoiling is a terrible experience for me with this setup (a lot of backfoot pressure, front foot pressure in non-existent). I will have to buy a new board that works with advance+ fuselage.

I used my first Fireball 1750 for one hour and a half. After this one session, I noticed it broke in the middle (cracks) in several parts, near the screws. Warranty has been accepted, and I received an new Fireball 1750.

I used this second Fireball 1750 for one hour. Exactly the same thing happened, except the crack was smaller this time, but still makes this very extreme wing unusable. Warranty has been accepted after a few months waiting.

I asked for a Fireball 1500 this time, hoping this one will be strong enough.

I used my PNG V2 1400 for dozens of sessions, tried beach starts with it, smashed it on rocks several times, and never had any structural cracks.
